old_claim.note: Measuring Rods & Lines provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: Kudurru evidence supports inscribed land-grant boundary monuments, not a direct dependency on measuring rods or field-survey instruments.

source_shape.source_locator: Description and curator comments: limestone boundary-stone prepared for sculptures and inscriptions, faces bearing inscription columns, and a charter from Nebuchadnezzar I.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The object page describes a boundary-stone with sculptures, inscriptions, and a charter rather than a measuring or surveying instrument.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The scoped artifact is a legal inscription monument. Measuring rods may belong to field surveying, but the cited evidence does not make them a direct antecedent for kudurru.

ontology_before: The graph let a general survey-tool capability feed directly into kudurru boundary stones, broadening the node beyond the museum evidence.
ontology_after: The graph scopes the node to inscribed kudurru land-grant monuments and removes the unsupported measuring-tool direct edge.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Graph invariant 2026-06-20-kudurru-boundary-stones-scope keeps measuring_rods_and_lines absent from the direct prerequisites.
would_reject_if: The node were renamed and sourced as a general field-survey marker technology rather than kudurru monuments.
A source showed that the earliest kudurru monuments depended on standardized measuring rods as a direct production or legal-record prerequisite.
short_reason: Kudurru are inscribed land-grant monuments, not measuring-rod survey technology.
